event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a sunlit view of a Parisian building, accompanied by gentle music, as Priscila Alexandre Spring, Hermès' Leather Goods Creative Director, introduces herself from Pantin, near Paris, inviting viewers to join her for the day. She speaks about Hermès' heritage and history, aiming for customers to experience 'love at first sight' and long-term ownership of their bags. Priscila, wearing a black coat over a denim shirt, walks through a garden path and enters a modern studio, marked 'ENTRÉE STUDIO,' with a distinct 'clunk' sound. Inside, she greets a colleague in a pink top in French, then describes the bright, organized leather goods studio as a space where the team works, noting the significance of opening their doors for the first time. She then leads viewers to her office, 'Priscila's Office,' a minimalist space with a white desk, laptop, tablet displaying a handbag sketch, design books, and flowers. While sketching on her tablet, Priscila shares that she grew up with a family that worked with their hands and that she has always loved to draw, illustrating her integration of digital and traditional design methods in a creative professional setting.

Priscila Alexandre Spring.description: Leather Goods Creative Director at Hermès, wearing a black coat over a denim shirt, with wavy brown hair, seen walking through a garden and entering her studio, later working on a tablet in her office. She introduces herself by name and title in a clear, slightly accented English voice, also speaking French to a colleague, and shares personal insights about her family and love for drawing.

Studio environment.description: A modern, well-lit workspace with glass walls, design tools, and creative materials, marked by a sign 'ENTRÉE STUDIO'. Priscila identifies it as "our leather goods studio where the team works" and mentions it's the "first time we open our door." A distinct "clunk" sound is heard upon entry.

Tablet sketch.description: A digital tablet displaying a handbag design sketch with annotations, used by Priscila for creative work.

Priscila's Office.description: A bright, minimalist office with a white desk, a laptop, a tablet, a stack of design books including 'HERMÈS ENARGIELA' and 'MAKING', and a colorful floral arrangement. Priscila explicitly states, "I'll take you to my office."

Colleague in pink top.description: A woman with short hair, wearing a pink top, working at a desk in the studio, handling design tools and materials. She is greeted by Priscila in French and responds in French, indicating she speaks French.

raw_segments: The video opens with a sweeping view of a Parisian building bathed in golden sunlight, set to gentle, uplifting background music. Priscila Alexandre Spring, the Leather Goods Creative Director at Hermès, introduces herself in a clear, slightly accented English voice, stating she's in Pantin, near Paris, and invites viewers to spend the day with her. She elaborates on Hermès' "strong heritage and a really beautiful history," expressing hope that customers experience "love at first sight" with their bags and will "hold for a long time." Priscila is then seen walking through a serene outdoor garden path, dressed in a black coat over a denim shirt. A distinct "clunk" sound accompanies her entry into a modern, glass-walled studio marked 'ENTRÉE STUDIO.' Inside, she warmly greets a colleague in a pink top in French ("Coucou, bonjour, ça va?"), who responds in kind. Priscila then identifies the bright, organized workspace, filled with design tools and materials, as "our leather goods studio where the team works," noting their excitement as "it's the first time we open our door." She then announces, "I'll take you to my office," leading into her bright, minimalist office, labeled 'Priscila's Office,' which features a white desk, a laptop, a tablet displaying a handbag design sketch, design books, and a vibrant floral arrangement. As she sketches on her tablet, Priscila reflects on her upbringing, stating, "I grew up in this environment of like with my family always working with their hands and myself I've always loved to draw," highlighting her blend of digital and traditional design processes within a professional yet creative atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, seated at a white desk in a bright, minimalist office, shared her childhood passion for sketching, inspired by her embroiderer grandmother and self-taught father, explaining her design process that begins with sketches, followed by marketeer discussions for 3D mock-ups before using leather. She demonstrated this by interacting with a digital tablet displaying a handbag design sketch, explaining the use of 'Salpa' to simulate leather for mock-ups. The scene transitioned to the Small Leather Goods Office where Louise was joined by a Colleague in a cream sweater and Henda, Head of Mens’ & Womens’ Small Leather Goods. Henda greeted Louise, and they discussed his work on an 'Hermes backpack.' Louise elaborated on their design philosophy, aiming for 'new gesturality' and 'more contemporary' elements while preserving 'recognizable codes of Hermes' and leveraging their 'strong heritage' for novelty. During this discussion, Henda presented a black leather pouch and a red hardware sample, while Louise held up a tan leather handbag, gesturing to emphasize points. The segment concluded with Louise and Henda exchanging handbags, symbolizing their collaborative design process.

Priscila's Office.description: A bright, minimalist office with a white desk, a laptop, a tablet, a stack of design books including 'HERMÈS ENARGIELA' and 'MAKING', and a colorful floral arrangement, visible throughout the segment.

Louise.description: Senior Leather Goods Designer, wearing a black long-sleeve top, brown trousers, gold jewelry, and dark nail polish, seated at a white desk in a bright office with shelves of books and design tools; she gestures while speaking and interacts with a tablet and sketches. Her clear, articulate voice explains the 'Salpa' material for prototyping and later discusses bringing 'new gesturality' and 'novelty' to Hermes collections, emphasizing their heritage.

Colleague in cream sweater.description: A woman with short, curly brown hair, wearing a cream ribbed sweater and white pants, carrying a white handbag with black leather accents and a black tote; she joins Louise for a design review, examining hardware samples and the handbag’s interior with focused attention.

Tablet sketch.description: A digital tablet displaying a handbag design sketch with annotations, used by Louise for creative work, shown in close-up as she interacts with it using her finger.

Black leather pouch.description: A small, black leather accessory with a flap closure and stitching details, presented by Henda during the design review.

Henda.description: Head of Mens’ & Womens’ Small Leather Goods, wearing a denim jacket and a black cap, presenting a small black leather pouch and a red hardware sample during the design review. His calm, clear voice is heard greeting Louise and discussing his work on the 'Hermes backpack'.

Red hardware sample.description: A red plastic or metal hardware component, possibly a buckle or connector, held by Henda to demonstrate design elements.

Small Leather Goods Office.description: A modern, well-lit studio space with large windows overlooking a cityscape, featuring design tools, plants, and a white table where collaborative design reviews take place; identified by on-screen text.

raw_segments: The video opens with Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, seated at a white desk in a bright, minimalist office, gesturing expressively as she speaks in a clear, articulate voice. She shares her childhood passion for sketching, inspired by her embroiderer grandmother who taught her the beauty of the reverse side of embroidery, and her self-taught creative father who engaged in painting and sculpture. Louise, wearing a black long-sleeve top, brown trousers, gold chain necklace, and a gold bracelet, with dark nail polish, explains her design process, starting with little sketches to convey ideas, which she then discusses with a marketeer to create a 3D mock-up before moving to leather. Behind her, black shelves display neatly stacked books, including 'MAKING' and 'HERMÈS ENARGIELA', alongside wooden speakers. The scene transitions to close-ups of hand-drawn sketches of a handbag’s hardware components, labeled 'format CLARÉ', followed by a digital tablet displaying a similar design, which Louise interacts with using her finger. The camera then pulls back to show her from behind, working at the desk with a laptop and tablet, near a colorful floral arrangement and large windows. A new woman, Louise's colleague, dressed in a cream ribbed sweater and white pants, enters carrying a white handbag with black leather accents and a black tote. Louise greets her warmly, and they engage in a friendly conversation, with the colleague asking in a clear voice about timing for a "show" and confirming samples, to which Louise responds positively. They sit together to examine the handbag, opening it to reveal its interior. They then lay out various hardware samples—black leather pieces with silver, rose gold, and gold metal clasps—on the table, discussing them closely, with the colleague inquiring about specific materials like "Salpa." The woman in cream leans in, inspecting the details with focused attention, while Louise points to specific elements, indicating a collaborative design review, all set to a backdrop of gentle, melodic music that begins towards the end of the segment.

The segment opens with a close-up of Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, interacting with a digital tablet displaying a handbag design sketch. Her clear, articulate voice explains the use of 'Salpa,' a fabric-like material that simulates leather, allowing for multiple mock-ups to visualize the final product before using real leather. Seated at a white desk in Priscila's Office, surrounded by design books, Louise gestures while speaking and then examines a physical white handbag prototype. A distinct 'thud' sound is heard as the scene transitions to the modern, well-lit Small Leather Goods Office. Here, Louise is joined by a Colleague in a cream sweater, and Henda, Head of Mens’ & Womens’ Small Leather Goods, wearing a denim jacket and black cap. Henda's calm, clear voice greets Louise, and they discuss his work on an 'Hermes backpack.' Louise then elaborates on the design philosophy, aiming for 'new gesturality' and 'more contemporary' elements while preserving the 'recognizable codes of Hermes.' She emphasizes approaching 'novelty' within their collections, leveraging their 'strong heritage' and 'beautiful history' to bring new ideas to the table based on their classics. During this discussion, Henda presents a small black leather pouch and a red hardware sample, and Louise holds up a tan leather handbag, gesturing to emphasize points. The segment concludes with Louise and Henda exchanging handbags, symbolizing their collaborative design process.

event_blocks.summary: In a modern, well-lit studio, Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, and Henda, Head of Mens’ & Womens’ Small Leather Goods, reviewed a black leather pouch and a red hardware sample, with Louise explaining that design inspiration for iconic bags like the Kelly and Birkin stems from old question catalogs, focusing on shape, strap, and form in an "endless" process. An elevator 'ding' preceded light laughter as Louise commented on the "Hermes lift" being "slow as well." Louise then moved through a glass corridor into the minimalist 'Bureau d’Études (Design Office),' a bright space overlooking a cityscape, to greet Christine, Director of the Bureau d’Études, who coordinates and manages artisans and is head of development, with all bags needing her validation. Christine, who has been with the company for "40 years," was described by Louise as knowing "everything." Later, in the Bureau d’Études, Louise and Christine, playfully called "Queen Christine" by Louise, approached Aurélie, a colleague in a green jacket and patterned scarf, at a workstation. Aurélie, using a wooden stitching stand and needle and thread, demonstrated precise hand-stitching techniques on a small leather component, explaining in French that it was assembled and ready for sewing, with Louise noting that Aurélie would stitch the new bag design, featuring a unique closure, by hand. The segment concluded with a close-up of a finished tan leather handbag with silver hardware, designed in the studio.

Black leather pouch.description: A small, black leather accessory with a flap closure and stitching details, presented by Henda during the design review.

Christine.description: Director of the Bureau d’Études, a woman with curly blonde hair, wearing a red top and black cardigan, standing beside a French flag and conversing with Louise in the design office. She is playfully referred to as 'Queen Christine' by Louise and acknowledges this title in French with a soft, appreciative tone.

Bureau d’Études (Design Office).description: A bright, open-plan design office with glass partitions, workstations, mannequins, and creative materials, marked by on-screen text and a French flag in the background. Sounds of general office chatter and a door opening/closing are heard as people move through the space.

Henda.description: Head of Mens’ & Womens’ Small Leather Goods, wearing a denim jacket and black cap, presenting a small black leather pouch and a red hardware sample during a design review.

Red hardware sample.description: A red plastic or metal hardware component, possibly a buckle or connector, held by Henda to demonstrate design elements.

Louise.description: Senior Leather Goods Designer, wearing a black long-sleeve top, brown trousers, gold jewelry, and dark nail polish, seen sketching, handling leather straps, and engaging in design discussions in a modern studio and office environment. She has a clear, articulate voice, speaking both French and English, and introduces the new bag design, explaining the stitching process and Aurélie's role.

Small Leather Goods Office.description: A modern, well-lit studio space with large windows overlooking a cityscape, featuring design tools, plants, and a white table where collaborative design reviews take place; identified by on-screen text.

French flag.description: A tricolor flag (blue, white, red) with a golden emblem, displayed prominently in the Bureau d’Études, symbolizing the French heritage of Hermès.

Tan leather handbag.description: A finished tan leather handbag with silver hardware, including a prominent clasp and strap, showcased at the end of the segment as an example of completed craftsmanship.

Leather stitching tools.description: A wooden stitching stand with multiple needles, a hand-held awl, and spools of thread, used by the colleague in the green jacket to demonstrate hand-stitching on leather.

Colleague in green jacket.description: A woman with short hair, wearing glasses and a green jacket with a patterned scarf, seated at a workstation and demonstrating hand-stitching techniques on a leather piece. She is identified by Louise as Aurélie, greets others in French, and speaks in a clear French accent, explaining that the leather piece is ready to be sewn and later confirming it's going well.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of hands meticulously sketching detailed horsebit-style leather goods designs on paper, as a female voice (later identified as Louise) explains how inspiration for designs, from the Kelly to the Birkin, comes from old question catalogs, focusing on shape, strap, and form, noting the process is "endless." The scene transitions to Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, in a black long-sleeve top and gold jewelry, interacting with Henda, Head of Mens’ & Womens’ Small Leather Goods, in a modern, well-lit studio. Henda presents a small black leather pouch and a red hardware sample for a collaborative design review. An elevator 'ding' is heard, followed by light laughter as Louise comments on the "Hermes lift" being "slow as well." Gentle, uplifting background music begins as Louise walks through a glass corridor into the 'Bureau d’Études (Design Office),' a bright, minimalist space overlooking a cityscape. She warmly greets Christine, Director of the Bureau d’Études, a woman with curly blonde hair in a red top, standing beside a French flag. Multiple voices exchange greetings in French and English, including Christine's, who responds "Oui, ça va?" Louise then introduces Christine, explaining she "coordinates and manages all the artisans" and is "head of development," ensuring "all the bags that leave Hermès... have to be validated by Christine." Christine's voice is heard asking in French, "Ça fait combien de temps tu es là?" (How long have you been here?), to which she replies, "Une petite quarantaine d'années" (40 years), further emphasized by Louise's voice stating, "She knows everything."

The video opens in a bright, open-plan design office, the Bureau d’Études, where Christine, its Director, stands beside a French flag, engaging in conversation with Louise, a Senior Leather Goods Designer. Louise playfully refers to Christine as “Queen Christine,” a title Christine acknowledges in French. As they walk through the modern workspace, filled with design tools and creative materials, sounds of office chatter and a door are heard. They approach a workstation where a colleague, identified by Louise as Aurélie, is seated, focused on a leather piece. Greetings are exchanged in French, with Aurélie responding in a clear voice. The trio gathers around the table, where Aurélie explains in French that the small leather component is assembled and ready to be sewn. Louise then elaborates in English on a new bag design, describing its unique closure and stating that Aurélie will stitch everything by hand. Close-ups show Aurélie, in her green jacket and patterned scarf, precisely demonstrating hand-stitching techniques on the leather using a needle and thread, with tools like a wooden stitching stand visible. The group observes attentively, accompanied by calm, instrumental background music, as the meticulous process unfolds. The segment culminates in a close-up of a finished tan leather handbag with silver hardware, showcasing the craftsmanship, as Louise concludes that the bag was designed in the studio. The atmosphere throughout is collaborative and focused, emphasizing the artistry involved in Hermès leather goods production.

event_blocks.summary: Louise, the Senior Leather Goods Designer, detailed the meticulous design process at Hermès, noting that while initial stages might take three months, the Bureau d’Études could extend development to six to nine months for perfection. In the bright, open-plan Bureau d’Études office, characterized by glass partitions and workstations, Louise, adorned in a black long-sleeve top, gold jewelry, and dark nail polish, animatedly discussed design specifics with Christine, Director of the Bureau d’Études, and a colleague sporting a green jacket and patterned scarf, with a French flag visible in the background. Transitioning to the workshop, Louise introduced Stéphane, a craftsman with long blonde hair and tattoos, and Alexandre from the Bureau des Affaires hardware workshop, highlighting their role in integrating metal and leather to create "the magic of this office." As they approached, greetings were exchanged in English and French, "Hello" and "Salut, ça va?" Stéphane demonstrated inserting a ring into leather, explaining in French to "completely slide it in" and then "insert the ring," while Alexandre observed. Louise further elaborated that the bag's design draws inspiration from a gigantic horse bit, aiming to conceal the metal part with leather, emphasizing Hermès' dedication to hand-polishing all brass hardware and valuing the "little details" like the distinct sounds of metal and closures, stating, "we love noise at Hermès." The scene concluded with a focus on the craftsmen's precise handiwork and a wide shot of the professional workshop, illuminated by natural light and equipped with neatly organized leatherworking tools such as chisels and awls.

Christine.description: Director of the Bureau d’Études, a woman with curly blonde hair, wearing a red top and black cardigan, standing beside a French flag and conversing with Louise in the design office.

Bureau d’Études (Design Office).description: A bright, open-plan design office with glass partitions, workstations, mannequins, and creative materials, marked by on-screen text and a French flag in the background. Mentioned by Louise as a key part of the design process and the source of 'the magic of this office'.

Alexandre.description: Craftsman in a light blue shirt and glasses, observing and assisting Stéphane during the leatherworking demonstration. He participates in the French conversation with Stéphane and Louise.

Louise.description: Senior Leather Goods Designer, wearing a black long-sleeve top, gold jewelry, and dark nail polish, actively engaging in discussions and demonstrating design elements in the Bureau d’Études. She is the primary narrator, speaking English with a clear, articulate voice, explaining design timelines, introducing craftsmen, and detailing Hermès' design philosophy, material processes, and attention to sensory details like sound.

Leatherworking tools.description: An array of precision tools including chisels, awls, and other leatherworking instruments neatly organized on wooden racks in the workshop, emphasizing the craftsmanship environment. The sounds of metal and closures are highlighted by Louise as important sensory details in Hermès products.

Stéphane.description: Craftsman with long blonde hair and tattoos, wearing a dark shirt, demonstrating a leatherworking technique involving inserting a ring into a leather piece. He speaks French, explaining the precise steps of the process.

Colleague in green jacket.description: A woman with short hair, wearing glasses and a green jacket with a patterned scarf, seated at a workstation and demonstrating hand-stitching techniques on a leather piece.

French flag.description: A tricolor flag (blue, white, red) with a golden emblem, displayed prominently in the Bureau d’Études, symbolizing the French heritage of Hermès.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a hand meticulously holding a red leather strap with a gold ring, emphasizing the exquisite craftsmanship of Hermès accessories. A female voice, identified as Louise, the Senior Leather Goods Designer, explains the design process, noting that while initial stages might take three months, the Bureau d’Études could extend development to six to nine months to perfect a piece. The scene transitions to the bright, open-plan Bureau d’Études, an office with glass partitions and workstations, where Louise, wearing a black long-sleeve top, gold jewelry, and dark nail polish, gestures expressively while discussing design details with Christine, Director of the Bureau d’Études, and a colleague in a green jacket with a patterned scarf. A French flag is visible in the background, emphasizing the brand's heritage. Louise then walks through the workshop, continuing her narration, explaining that they are about to see Stéphane, a craftsman with long blonde hair and tattoos, working with Alexandre, a craftsman in a light blue shirt and glasses, from the Bureau des Affaires, the hardware workshop. She highlights their task of integrating metal and leather to create "the magic of this office." As Louise approaches, greetings are exchanged in both English and French ("Hello," "Salut, ça va?"), indicating a friendly, collaborative atmosphere. Stéphane, demonstrating the technique of inserting a ring into a leather piece, explains in French how to "completely slide it in" and then "insert the ring," with Alexandre observing and participating in the conversation. Louise's voice returns, detailing that the bag's design is inspired by a gigantic horse bit, with the goal of concealing the metal part with leather. She emphasizes Hermès' commitment to hand-polishing all hardware and using brass, adding that they "love noise at Hermès," referring to the distinct sounds of metal and closures, which are "little details that we pay a lot of attention" to. The camera focuses on the craftsmen's hands as they meticulously work, before a wide shot reveals the professional workshop, filled with neatly organized leatherworking tools like chisels and awls, bathed in natural light, underscoring the precision and artistry of Hermès.

event_blocks.summary: In the bustling Atelier Harmonie, Karine, a craftswoman, explained the intricate details of leatherwork, emphasizing the importance of listening to the sounds of the craft and demonstrating the precise creation of a small hole for a closure on a blue leather piece, securing a silver hardware component with metallic tapping sounds, noting the four years of training and meticulous 'perlage' technique requiring redoing the entire process for any mistake. A voice-over commended her with 'Bravo Karine.' Later, Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, explained that craftsman Fabrice was performing the final stitching on a Kelly bag, a process taking around 13 hours, as he meticulously attached a metal clasp to a black leather item with pliers. Louise detailed the Kelly bag's history as an 'archetype' from the 1930s, famously named after Grace Kelly, and its intricate 'inside out' construction requiring significant skill, before showcasing a completed black leather handbag. Transitioning to a storage area with rolls of colorful leather, Louise explained their 'stock of leathers' for 'mockups,' boasting about 200 colors and 50 materials. The scene then shifted to Marc, a craftsman, carefully aligning and securing a metal clasp onto a black leather bag on a green cutting mat. Louise, in the 'Bureau d’Études' area, emphasized that craftsmanship, a hand-done work transmitted through generations with specific 'savoir-faire' in stitching and assembly, is at the core of the Maison, with Hermès bags designed for longevity and sustainability, repairable even after 30 years. She introduced Marc, who has worked at Hermès for 25 years and was assembling a 'Birkin Picnic' bag, combining leather and wicker components by meticulously attaching a metal handle and threading a leather strap through a metal loop with precision.

Atelier Harmonie (Harmony Workshop).description: A well-lit, spacious leathercraft workshop with multiple workstations, tools, and materials, featuring a green cutting mat, leather rolls, and a sign reading 'Bureau d’Études' in the background, emphasizing a skilled and collaborative environment.

Karine.description: Craftswoman with wavy brown hair, wearing a dark long-sleeve top and a gold necklace, who speaks clearly and instructively in both English and French, explaining intricate leathercraft techniques and engaging in conversation. Her voice is heard describing the process of securing hardware and the extensive training required for artisans.

Black leather item.description: A dark leather piece, possibly a bag or pouch, being worked on by Marc, featuring a metal clasp being attached with careful handiwork.

Fabrice.description: Craftsman with short dark hair, wearing a grey long-sleeve shirt under a black vest, meticulously attaching a metal clasp to a black leather item using a small tool, demonstrating the 'lot of skill' required for the 'inside out' construction of a Kelly bag, as explained by Louise.

Metal hardware component.description: A silver metal piece, likely a clasp or connector, being inserted and secured into the black leather item by Marc using a precision tool.

Colleague in black hoodie.description: A woman with short blonde hair and glasses, wearing a black hoodie, seated and observing Karine’s demonstration attentively in the workshop.

Blue leather piece.description: A rectangular blue leather item with a metal hardware component being assembled by Karine, accompanied by distinct metallic tapping sounds as the hardware is inserted and secured, demonstrating detailed craftsmanship.

Black leather handbag.description: A completed black leather 'Kelly bag' with silver hardware, including a prominent clasp and handle, showcased by Louise as an example of finished craftsmanship, embodying the historical 'archetype' she describes.

Louise.description: Senior Leather Goods Designer, wearing a dark long-sleeve top, gold jewelry, and dark nail polish, actively speaking with a clear French accent and expressive hands in the Atelier Harmonie. She explains the core philosophy of Hermès craftsmanship, longevity, and sustainability, and later introduces Marc, while holding and examining a tan leather handbag.

Blue leather roll.description: A roll of blue leather, one of 'about 200 colors' and '50 materials' from the extensive 'stock of leathers' used for 'mockups' at the Atelier, selected by Louise from a storage shelf.

Woven basket with leather trim.description: A light-colored woven basket with leather accents, being assembled by Marc, featuring a metal handle being attached with precision and care. It is identified by Louise as a 'Birkin Picnic' bag, involving the assembly of leather and wicker.

Bureau d’Études sign.description: A red and white sign with the text 'Bureau d’Études' visible in the background of the workshop, indicating the design office space and reinforcing the French heritage of the craft.

Tan leather handbag.description: A finished tan leather handbag with silver hardware, including a prominent clasp and strap, showcased by Louise as an example of completed craftsmanship.

Marc.description: Craftsman with a beard and glasses, wearing a black turtleneck, meticulously attaching metal hardware to a woven basket with leather trim, using precision tools and demonstrating craftsmanship. He is introduced by Louise as having worked at Hermès for 25 years, and is seen assembling a 'Birkin Picnic' bag, combining leather and wicker components.

Green cutting mat.description: A textured green mat used as a work surface in the workshop, visible beneath the black leather item during hardware assembly, providing contrast and protection for the materials.

raw_segments: The minute opens in a well-lit, bustling leathercraft atelier, identified by on-screen text as 'Atelier Harmonie (Harmony Workshop),' where Karine, a craftswoman with wavy brown hair, wearing a dark long-sleeve top and a gold necklace, speaks in English about the intricate details of the craft that most people don't know. She emphasizes the importance of listening to and liking the sounds of the work. Karine then greets someone in French, followed by a colleague with short blonde hair and glasses, wearing a black hoodie, attentively observing her demonstration. Karine continues in English, explaining the front of a 'Kelly' bag and the process of creating a small hole for a closure. As she demonstrates the assembly of a blue leather piece, using a metal tool to secure a silver hardware component, distinct metallic tapping sounds are heard, highlighting the precision of her craftsmanship. She elaborates on the four years of training required for artisans and the meticulous 'perlage' technique, stressing that even a single mistake necessitates redoing the entire process. A voice-over then commends her with 'Bravo Karine.' The scene transitions to another craftsman, Fabrice, identified by on-screen text, with short dark hair, wearing a grey long-sleeve shirt under a black vest, meticulously attaching a metal clasp to a black leather item. Karine greets Fabrice in French, and he responds, confirming their interaction. Throughout the segment, a gentle, orchestral background music plays, complementing the focused atmosphere of skilled manual labor and artisanal quality.

The video opens in the bustling Atelier Harmonie, where Senior Leather Goods Designer Louise, wearing a dark long-sleeve top and gold jewelry, speaks directly to the camera in a clear, articulate voice, explaining that craftsman Fabrice is performing "the last stitching on a Kelly bag," a process that takes "around 13 hours." As Fabrice meticulously attaches a metal clasp to a black leather item with pliers, Louise elaborates on the Kelly bag's history, calling it an "archetype" from the 1930s, famously named after Grace Kelly. She further details the bag's intricate "inside out" construction, emphasizing that it "requires a lot of skill." Louise then holds up a completed black leather handbag with silver hardware, showcasing the finished product. The scene transitions to a well-lit storage area filled with rolls of colorful leather, as elegant classical piano music begins to play. Louise walks through the shelves, selecting a roll of blue leather, and her voice returns to explain that this is their "stock of leathers" used for "mockups," boasting "about 200 colors" and "50 materials" always in collection.

The video opens with a close-up of a hand placing a gold and red hardware piece onto a black leather item, as soft, reflective orchestral music begins to play. The scene transitions to Marc, a craftsman in a red sweater, carefully aligning and securing a metal clasp onto a black leather bag using a tool on a green cutting mat. Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, then appears in the well-lit Atelier Harmonie, filled with leather rolls and a 'Bureau d’Études' sign. Speaking with a clear French accent and expressive hands, Louise explains that craftsmanship is at the core of the Maison, a hand-done work transmitted through generations, emphasizing their specific 'savoir-faire' in stitching and assembly. She highlights that Hermès bags address longevity and sustainability from the very beginning, designed to be repairable even after 30 years, aiming for them to last as long as possible. As she speaks, she holds and examines a tan leather handbag, showcasing its structure and stitching. The music continues as Louise introduces Marc, noting he has worked at Hermès for 25 years and is currently working on a 'Birkin Picnic' bag. The camera focuses on Marc, a craftsman with a beard and glasses, meticulously attaching a metal handle to the woven basket with leather trim, using a small tool to secure the hardware. Louise explains he is assembling the bag, combining the leather and wicker components. Close-ups show Marc’s hands threading a leather strap through a metal loop and securing it with precision, demonstrating the skilled, collaborative nature of the leathercraft process in the organized workshop.

event_blocks.summary: In the well-lit Atelier Harmonie, craftsman Marc, a bearded man in glasses and a black turtleneck, meticulously attaches a metal handle to a woven basket with leather trim using precision tools, with organized leatherworking tools, a green cutting mat, and a 'Bureau d’Études' sign visible in the background as gentle, melancholic piano music plays. Louise, speaking with a slight accent, explains that the lacing takes about 8 hours and the whole bag 19 hours, noting Marc has already started the interior lacing. The scene shifts to a display of finished Hermès handbags, including a tan and a black leather handbag with silver hardware, showcasing signature craftsmanship, while the piano music continues. Transitioning to a modern studio environment, Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, stands beside a white table, holding the tan leather handbag. Dressed in a dark long-sleeve top with gold jewelry and dark nail polish, her wavy brown hair styled loosely, she addresses the camera, expressing hope that Vogue had a good day learning about Hermès' processes and wishing them farewell as it's nighttime. Behind her, shelves display design books like 'HERMÈS ENARGIELA' and 'MAKING', alongside decorative objects and a small brown handbag. The segment concludes with black screen credits listing production roles and the Vogue Video Team as the piano music fades out.

Atelier Harmonie (Harmony Workshop).description: A well-lit, spacious leathercraft workshop with multiple workstations, tools, and materials, featuring a green cutting mat, leather rolls, and a sign reading 'Bureau d’Études' in the background, emphasizing a skilled and collaborative environment.

Studio environment.description: A modern, well-lit workspace with glass walls, design tools, and creative materials, marked by a sign 'ENTRÉE STUDIO'.

Woven basket with leather trim.description: A light-colored woven basket with leather accents, being assembled by Marc, featuring a metal handle being attached with precision and care. Louise describes the extensive time required for its lacing (8 hours) and the entire bag's completion (19 hours).

She has a clear voice with a slight accent, explaining the intricate process of bag making and later addressing "Vogue" to conclude the segment.

His work is described by Louise, who mentions him by name, stating he "already started inside" on the lacing of the bag.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a close-up of craftsman Marc, a bearded man in glasses and a black turtleneck, meticulously attaching a metal handle to a woven basket with leather trim using precision tools in the well-lit Atelier Harmonie. Organized leatherworking tools, a green cutting mat, and a 'Bureau d’Études' sign are visible in the background. Gentle, melancholic piano music plays as a female voice, later identified as Louise, explains with a slight accent that "It takes about 8 hours to do the lacing all around the bag, as you can see Marc already started inside, and 19 hours to do the whole bag." The scene transitions to a display of finished Hermès handbags, including a tan and a black leather handbag with silver hardware, showcasing signature craftsmanship, while the piano music continues. The camera then shifts to a modern studio environment where Louise, Senior Leather Goods Designer, stands beside a white table, holding the tan leather handbag and speaking to the camera. Dressed in a dark long-sleeve top with gold jewelry and dark nail polish, her wavy brown hair styled loosely, she continues, "Okay Vogue, uh, I hope you spent a good day today with us here at Hermès. I hope you learned a lot about how we do things, and it's night time, so time to go home. See you soon." Behind her, shelves display design books like 'HERMÈS ENARGIELA' and 'MAKING', alongside decorative objects and a small brown handbag. The segment concludes with black screen credits listing production roles such as Director Nikki Petersen, Director of Photography Étienne Baussan, Editor Evan Allan, and the Vogue Video Team, as the piano music fades out.

global_entity_library.Priscila Alexandre Spring: Leather Goods Creative Director at Hermès, wearing a black coat over a denim shirt, with wavy brown hair, seen walking through a garden and entering her studio, later working on a tablet in her office. She introduces herself by name and title in a clear, slightly accented English voice, also speaking French to a colleague, and shares personal insights about her family and love for drawing.
